coredata.dc:title: NS-CQDs/TiO2 hybrid nanocomposites incorporated with nutshell derived NS-CQDs as photocatalyst for environmental/biological applications and cytotoxicity effect

coredata.dc:description: Being a class of carbon nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been used as a precursor in modification of semiconductor photocatalysts. The efficient utilization of natural resources ‘‘biomass’’ for the synthesis of CQDs which are important for reducing environmental pollution, and also creates a field of application area such as water treatment. In this work, fix-bed pyrolysis reactor was used to synthesis the bright fluorescence NS-CQDs using nutshell (NS) without any chemicals. One-dimensional TiO2 nanorods (TNRs) fabricated via hydrothermal route was decorated with low-cost and eco-friendly (NS-CQDs) by wet impregnation method to produce hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs nanocomposite. Various characterization techniques such as TGA, XRD, XPS, FE-SEM/EDS, HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy have been employed to confirm the structural and optical properties. The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared NS-CQDs/TNRs were investigated for methylene blue (MB), hexavalent chromium Cr (VI), Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Aspergillus niger (A. niger) under UV-A. Subsequently, MTT assay was carried out using ARPE-19 (Epithelium) and MCF-7 (Breast Cancer) cell lines as the model system so as to designate whether produced NS-CQDs and hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs composite was cytotoxic or not. The obtained results suggest that the photocatalytic activity of NS-CQDs/TNRs composites was slightly enhanced as compared to that of TNRs while not only NS-CQDs but also NS-CQDs/TNRs composites show no significant change in terms of cytotoxicity.

head.ce:title: NS-CQDs/TiO2 hybrid nanocomposites incorporated with nutshell derived NS-CQDs as photocatalyst for environmental/biological applications and cytotoxicity effect

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Being a class of carbon nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been used as a precursor in modification of semiconductor photocatalysts. The efficient utilization of natural resources ‘‘biomass’’ for the synthesis of CQDs which are important for reducing environmental pollution, and also creates a field of application area such as water treatment. In this work, fix-bed pyrolysis reactor was used to synthesis the bright fluorescence NS-CQDs using nutshell (NS) without any chemicals. One-dimensional TiO2 nanorods (TNRs) fabricated via hydrothermal route was decorated with low-cost and eco-friendly (NS-CQDs) by wet impregnation method to produce hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs nanocomposite. Various characterization techniques such as TGA, XRD, XPS, FE-SEM/EDS, HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy have been employed to confirm the structural and optical properties. The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared NS-CQDs/TNRs were investigated for methylene blue (MB), hexavalent chromium Cr (VI), Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Aspergillus niger (A. niger) under UV-A. Subsequently, MTT assay was carried out using ARPE-19 (Epithelium) and MCF-7 (Breast Cancer) cell lines as the model system so as to designate whether produced NS-CQDs and hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs composite was cytotoxic or not. The obtained results suggest that the photocatalytic activity of NS-CQDs/TNRs composites was slightly enhanced as compared to that of TNRs while not only NS-CQDs but also NS-CQDs/TNRs composites show no significant change in terms of cytotoxicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid growth in global population and unplanned industry expansion is to lead the mounting environmental pollutants that contribute to significant health problems, biodiversity loss and destruction of natural habitat for continuity of humankind and other all living creatures [1,2]. In order to removal the various organic, inorganic, and pathogenic species that are harmful to human and ecological health, photocatalysis by metal oxide semiconductors is a green, clean and effective methods that most widely used in wastewater treatment of all kinds of pollutants besides other potential application area [3,4].

Sunlight-induced photocatalysis ‘‘Heterogeneous photocatalysis’’ as a green approach operates a solid semiconductor photocatalyst to adsorb solar light in generating electron-hole (
e
−
-
h
+
) pairs, which take charge in total oxidation or reduction of most of the pollutants [5]. TiO2 among various nanostructured metal-oxide based photocatalyst to remove the pollutants in contaminated water is frequently preferred owing to wide electronic band gap (
E
g
= ∼ 3.2 eV
for anatase structure) as well as high stability, cost-effective, excellent catalytic activity, nontoxicity and reusability [6]. Nonetheless, being a wide band gap in the UV region and high rate recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, TiO2 semiconductor gives rise to poor photocatalytic removal efficiency and constraining following inquiries a variety of sector [7]. It has known that a great amount of operative initiatives, which could foster maximum utilization of the visible and near infrared (NIR) region of the solar spectrum and keep under control separation of photoinduced charges, has fabricated to come through these impediments [4]. A heap of strategies such as dye sensitization, surface modification, metal or nonmetal doping, and heterojunction formation have been performed to manufacture optimized TiO2 nanostructures so as to improve optical absorption and minimizes recombination rate [5,7].

Even though it is mostly carried out denoted efforts, it is essential to devised the novel morphologies of the TiO2 nanomaterials beside surface, optical, and electronic properties, so another method is that it is changed the geometry of TiO2 catalyst [8]. In recent of decades, it has been indicated that geometry of TiO2 nanoparticles significantly acts on photocatalytic performance of catalyst over the pollutants located in aquatic systems. Especially, one dimensional (1D) morphologies such as nanorod, nanotube, nanofiber, nanowire and so on structures have been built so as to enhance the photocatalytic effect of TiO2 particles [9]. TiO2 nanorods (TNRs) among the 1D structures have drawn attention as preeminent types because of direct and fast flow of photogenerated charges within one dimension and large surface area, thereby increasing light absorption [3,8,10].

Generally, photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanostructures can be dramatically developed with deposition of different metal/nonmetal/noble metals as a co-catalyst on the TiO2 surface by forming an interfacial Schottky barrier. But, it is required to find out a new generation co-catalysts to decrease expenditure and increase range of potential applications [11].

With the advances in fluorescent materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composed to sp2/sp3 carbon domain have known as a famous new zero-dimensional (0D) quasi-spherical particles < 10 nm in size [12]. It becomes promising candidate materials for the modification of TiO2 owing to optical properties and nanosized morphology such as their tunable emission, high light stability, suitable band gap, water solubility, and unique electron donor/acceptor properties when compared to other fluorescent substances [13,14]. Nowadays, biomass as starting materials of CQDs is recognized due to possessed naturally abundant, environmental friendliness, non-toxic, renewable and sustainability, and low-cost although chemical toxic reagents are particularly utilized as initializer in synthesizing of CQDs [15–17].

There are many studies that used CQDs as co-catalysts to improve the various new types of semiconductors-based materials. Atchudan et al. developed new hybrid materials consisting of TiO2 nanoparticles functionalized with CQDs generated from peach juice as a natural green source that produced TiO2 NPs/C nanoparticles evaluated in the photocatalytic removal of methylene blue as well as Candida albicans cell. It has reported that synergistic effect between TiO2 NPs and CQDs in new type nanocomposite was increased removal rate on the pollutants [18]. Lignin-derived carbon quantum dots/nickel metal-organic layer (Ni-MOL) heterojunction was used by [19] to investigate photocatalytic tetracycline degradation. Also, Heng et al., published the use of waste palm frond as a precursor to synthesize CQDs, which enhanced the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 nanoparticles because of holding electron mediator and visible light harvester [20]. It has been demonstrated that CQDs synthesized by one-step microwave assisted solvothermal route of orange peels as raw materials provided red-shift in the energy band gap and an increase of the charge transfer in the TiO2 lattice structure [21]. Jun Park and Kyoung Yang have utilized coffee residues, which are abundant everywhere, to produce the biomass derived CQDs for environmental remediation [22]. Similarly, CQDs prepared from spent coffee grounds as biomass carbon sources to design the CQDs/TiO2 were developed for the absorption of visible light, and simplified electron transfer and separation of photo-generated (
e
−
-
h
+
) pairs [23]. Another study found that TiO2 decorated with microalgae based CQDs exhibited a superior photodegradation of methylene blue [24]. Tuna Genç and co-worker have fabricated CQDs/TiO2 composites by introducing CQDs manufactured from Gingko biloba by using microwave technique [25]. In literature research, Camellia oleifera derived carbon dots modified TiO2 nanocomposite generated by Zhan and Xu were used as a potential sensor for the rapid and accurate detection of 4-nitrophenol in wastewater [26]. In a spectacular study in the literature, antimicrobial effect of CQDs modified TiO2 nanorod has been tested against Staphylococcus aureus, then cytotoxicity tests have been performed on bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells [27]. The inactivation of Gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria as models have been realized on a CQDs/TiNT films [28]. Different concentrations of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CQDs composite particles have been investigated against mouse peritoneal macrophages, the experimental data have revealed that composite particles show obvious low cytotoxicity to the mouse peritoneal macrophages [29]. Fabricated Fe3O4, Ti3+-TiO2, and TiO2 nanocomposites with immobilization of CQDs generated from biomass or chemical reagents with different synthesize methods have been used in photo-inactivation of bacteria and fungi, and on various cell lines for cytotoxicity study [30–32].

Presented this work highlights the synthesis of NS-CQDs from nutshell powder as bio-waste with pyrolysis route by using fix-bed pyrolysis reactor, and utilization of their as a co-catalyst was performed with wet impregnation techniques in modification of TiO2 nanorods (TNRs). To demonstrate the practical photocatalytic applications, synthesized and characterized NS-CQDs/TNRs were evaluated and discussed in the removal of methylene blue (MB), hexavalent chromium Cr (VI) as well as photo-inactivation of Escherichia coli (E.coli) and Aspergillus Niger (A.Niger). The importance of the current work lies in the fact that synergistic effect occurred by penetrating of NS-CQDs in the TNRs structure regulated the electron transferring though channels that can be separated of (
e
−
-
h
+
) pairs, and rising up the photocatalytic removal rate by mostly utilizing from visible light. Another important is that in addition to the idea of reusing biomass accumulated in nature for the synthesis of CQDs, it is also a creative approach to environmentally friendly remove these wastes from nature. In this study, nutshell, which are both agricultural and industrial effluents that emerge after taking away nutmeat from nut used as effective organic waste to synthesize the CQDs. More importantly, it was demonstrated that modification of TNRs with NS-CQDs was effected green- inexpensively to execute MB degradation, removal of Cr (VI), and inactivation of E.coli/A.niger. Finally, after then photocatalytic experiments, cytotoxicity of both NS-CQDs and NS-CQDs/TNRs were tested for ARPE-19 (human retinal epithelial) and MCF-7 (Breast Cancer) cell lines.

NS were collected from Yeşilbük Village, Trabzon in Turkey. As the complexing agent, 1,5-Diphenyl Carbazide (C13H14ON4) was obtained from LaboChemie. Commercial TiO2 (anatase) nanoparticles (≥99 %), methylene blue, acetone, sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥99 %) were bought by Sigma Aldrich. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) was supplied from Carlo Erba Company. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, ≥99.9 %) was purchased from AppliChem (Panreac ITW Companies). With a 6 W (X2) dual lamp, irradiation source emitting radiation at 254/365 nm (light intensity 350 mW/cm2) was acquired from Spectro line ENF260. Müeller Hinton Agar (MHB) and Sabaroud Dextroz Agar (SDA) were purchased from Merck Millipore. These reagents were of analytical purity grades. Phosphate buffer (PBS) saline was supplied from Pan Biotech firm. In the further purification of NS-CQDs was used Serva brand as a dialysis bag. E. coli (ATCC 25922) and isolates of A. niger were procured from the Karadeniz Technical University, Department of Medical Microbiology and Hacettepe University, Department of Medical Microbiology, Mycology laboratory. Deionized water was supplied by a Merck Millipore Direct-Q® 8 UV for the whole solution preparations though photocatalytic applications. Ultrasonic bath is used Bandelin Sonorex Digitec. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Fresh distilled water (DW) was used throughout all tests, experiments, treatments, and rinsing. Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) was supplied from Pan Biotech. 3,4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-l)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT dye) and trypan blue solution were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DEMEM), Fetal bovine serum (FBS), and penicillin/streptomycin were supplied from Thermo. Tripsin-EDTA was supplied from Wisent.

In order to characterize, it was applied TGA, XRD, Uv–vis absorbance spectroscopy, XPS, FE-SEM/EDS, HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, and UV–vis DRS analyses during the experiments. FTIR spectra of the photocomposites was recorded by a Perkin Elmer 1600 FTIR spectrometer by scanning from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The optical properties of NS-CQDs/TNRs photocatalysts were recorded by UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV 3600 Plus) in which BaSO4 was used as the reflectance standard material. The crystal structure of as-prepared photocatalysts was characterized on a PANalytical Empyrean XRD using a diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154056 nm) (60 kV, 100 mA) in the range of 10°-90°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were surveyed by Specs-Flex XPS with monochromated Al Kα radiation to analyze the elementary composition and the chemical structure of the NS-CQDs/TNRs and pristine TNRs. All fluorescence spectra of NS-CQDs liquid samples were acquired by a Photon Technologies International Quanta Master-Spectrofluorimeter (model QM-4/2006) spectrofluorometer with a slit width of 5.0 nm, with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 400 V and scanning speed of 1200 nm/min at room temperature. In an attempt to determine the morphology and particle size distributions of dot samples and composites, HRTEM and FE-SEM along with EDS images were collected using FEI TALOS F200S TEM (200 kV) and FEI Quanta 650 field emission SEM operated an speeding up voltage of 100 V-30 kV, respectively (Resolution: 1.2 nm@30 kV (SED)/0.8 nm@30 kV (STEM)), and EDS images were collected by an Inca 250 SSD XMax20 detector which is Peltier cooled with 20 mm2 active area and 129 eV resolution used for elemental analysis. The pyrolysis characteristics of the NS were conceded using an Exstar SII TG/DTA 3600 instrument under a N2 atmosphere in the heating temperature in the range of 25–600 °C and the heating rate was controlled at 8 °C/min. It was used 4.63 mg samples and particle size around 200 mesh. Finally, the flow rate of the carrier gas was fixed at 50 mL/min (pan: alumina). The carbonization process of 4 g of NS powders was carried out with a fix-bed pyrolysis reactor at 320 °C at a constant heating rate of 5 °C/min under nitrogen atmosphere by ensuring a reaction time of 2 hours. UV–visible absorption spectra were collected by Analytik Jena/Specord 210 plus using quartz cuvettes of an optical path length of 10 mm, in the spectral range of 200–600 nm, with a slid width of 2 nm both determine the optical properties of NS-CQDs liquid samples and calculate the removal of MB and Cr (VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NS-CQDs liquid solution and pyrolysis of nutshell
ce:section.ce:para: The process of preparing pyrolyzed NS and synthesis of NS-CQDs solution is presented in Fig. 1.

Experimental processes of slow pyrolysis were carried out in vertical fixed-bed reactor used for carbonization (internal diameter of 10 mm and 700 mm length). NS powder, which was well-grounded with laboratory type pulverizing ring mill was loaded into the reactor, after providing appropriate carbonization (biomass powder, heating rate, temperature, carrier gas, and gas flow rate) conditions, pyrolysis was maintained at the selected temperature for 2 hours. Pyrolysis temperature was determined at 320 °C, which is mid-point temperature verified experimentally by TG/DTG analysis. More detailed data on pyrolysis and information on the preparation of aqueous dots after pyrolysis were presented in our previous study [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The TNRs were synthesized though a simple hydrothermal technique [33]. Briefly, 1 g of commercial anatase TiO2 was pour in to 80 mL of concentrated 10 M NaOH solution stirred at room temperature until dispersion was occurred. In the aftermath of dispersion, mixture was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h in an oven. At last, the 1D TNRs were calcined at 400 °C for 4 h in air following washing and centrifugation.

In the second step for synthesis of the NS-CQDs-NTRs heterostructured photocomposites ( Fig. 2), it was kept track of easy dispersion procedure in which 0.1 g of the fabricated white 1D TNRs were dispersed with five different volumes of NS-CQDs from 1 mL to 25 mL according to the same experimental procedure, and obtained suspension was topped- up the volume to 100 mL by adding deionize water. Subsequently, the suspension was sonicated with ultrasonic bath for 30 minutes (at 15 min. intervals). The mixture was allowed to stir for 3 h at room temperature, followed by drying in the laboratory oven at 80 °C to evaporate deionize water. The obtained NS-CQDs/TNTs composites with different volumes of CQDs are labelled to be NS-CQDs(X)/TNTs, where x corresponds to volume of NS-CQDs solution, which added 1 mL, 5 mL, 10 mL, 15 mL, 20 mL, and 10 mL [33]. The final white one dimensional NS-CQDS(X)/TNTs powders were kept using for the required characterizations, further photocatalytic applications and cytotoxicity tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation method of photocatalytic removal of MB and Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
To prepare the samples for photocatalytic activity testing, 0.1 g/L NS-CQDs(X)/TNRs under UV-A light was dispersed in an aqueous solution of 10 mg/L K2Cr2O7 and MB as precursor pollutants, and the dispersion was then kept for 30 min under darkness to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium at room temperature. Aqueous MB and Cr (VI) solution including the NS-CQDs(X)/TNRs photocomposite in a 100 mL quartz cell was placed at roughly 10 cm, and then exposed for 120 and 150 min (MB, Cr (VI) resp.) under UV-A (365 nm). The Cr(VI) solution was set at a pH: 1.5 using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH. After that the photocatalytic reaction, 5 mL aliquots of mixed MB and Cr (VI) solution at certain intervals was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and the concentration of residual contaminants were evaluated with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 540 and 664 nm, which are characteristic absorption peaks (
λ
max
) of Cr (VI) and MB. The change in absorption of Cr(VI) was monitored via the 1,5-dipenylcarbazide method [34] in acid solution. The removal percentage (%) of Cr (VI) and MB were calculated using Eq. 1 [35]
(1)

are absorbance at initial time and final absorbance of the end of irradiation time, respectively.

To investigate the reusability and stability of the prepared NS-CQDs/TNR after photodegradation and photoreduction process were carried out regeneration experiments. Firstly, composites were separated by centrifugation after every photoremoval experiments, and then rinsed in deionized water. For MB; washed composites were rewashed with ethanol until MB adsorbed on the composites were completely desorbed back in to the aqueous solution, and dried at 75 °C overnight. As for Cr (VI), it was applied same procedure that of MB regeneration, but one difference that so as to completely remove the absorbed Cr(III) on the catalysts surface, it was immersed into 1 M HNO3. The cycling experiments were performed under the same experimental conditions as mentioned above, which also this procedure was repeated from each cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photokilling measurements for E-coli and A. niger
ce:section.ce:para: In the antibacterial and antifungal experiment, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Aspergillus niger (A. niger) as pathogens were selected to assess the antimicrobial activity of the investigated NS-CQDs(X)/TNRs under visible light. The photo-inactivation tests used fresh strains of E. coli and A. niger were cultured according to previous studies [33]. The densities for the tested pathogens was adjusted to 103-108 CFU/mL (0.5 McFarland). 1 g/L of the NS-CQDs(X)/TNRs composite was suspended in 20 mL of microbial cell suspensions, and mixture was gently stirred (500 rpm) throughout the illumination period 150 min. Every 30 min for sampling, pipetted 10 µL of aliquots was uniformly spread onto the MHB agar plates using a sterile glass rod. Following another 24 h and 28 h incubation period at 37 °C for bacterial and fungal viability, resp., plates were photographed in order to monitor the inactivation of pathogenic species. It should be noted that microorganism suspensions were used by diluting 5000-fold for E. coli and 1000-fold for A. niger before starting photocatalytic inactivation experiments. All experiments were repeated three times and equipment’s used in photokilling assays were autoclaved at 121 °C for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in-vitro cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The MTT assay protocol was used to analyze whether the synthesized NS-CQDs and hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs composite have toxic effects against [36,37]. Breast cancer (MCF-7, ATCC HTB-22) cells and human retinal epithelial (ARPE-19, ATCC) cells. Both cells were cultured in DMEM with penicillin/streptomycin (1 %, v/v) and FBS (10 %, v/v). These cells, which will be used for cytotoxicity determination, were incubated in a humidified environment at 37 °C containing 5 % CO2. ARPE-19 and MCF-7 cells propagated in T-75 flasks were detached using trypsin. 5000 cells were added to each well of a 96-well plate in 200 μL of medium and then placed in an incubator. After 24 hours, the media in the plates were replaced with fresh medium. The NS-CQDs and hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs composite were added to the wells to achieve final concentrations of 0 (control), 15,6; 31,25; 62,5; 125; 250 and 500 μg/mL. The plates were then incubated at 37°C for 48 hours. After incubation, MTT method was applied. The absorbance of the resulting purple color was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader spectrophotometer. The relative cell viability for each sample was calculated by dividing the absorbance of the sample by the absorbance of the control and multiplying by 100. For each cell series, the cell viability was calculated using the following formula (Eq. 2):[37]
(2)

Thermogravimetric analysis was performed to determine the optimum thermal of carbonization and pyrolysis behavior of NS samples. Fig. 3 presents a detailed thermogravimetric curves of (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) for NS samples.

The thermal decomposition profile of NS is formed from two different stages. The initial peak appeared range of 25–115 °C in the decomposition of NS indicates the elimination of small amount of water and adsorbed volatiles. Roughly 10.87 % of initial mass loss occurred at the remarked temperature. The maximum mass loss of NS decomposition was found between 115 °C and 375 °C, the majority of which is pyrolysis of cellulose and hemicellulose components. Observed weight loss conform to results obtained from other studies, reporting that pyrolysis temperature is surged, components into the biomass such as cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin carbonizes by the way of dehydration and fractionation [38,39]. Pyrolysis temperature (320 °C) was selected from mid-point decomposition temperature of NS obtained from exothermic peak of DTG runs, indicating that particularly hemicellulose and cellulose as components of biomass were substantially decomposition [40].

To understand the optical features of the fabricated NS-CQDs aqueous solution was performed the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectral analyses. While NS-CQDs aqueous solution appears fawn-colored under daylight, when irradiated under UV light (365 nm), it emits silver-blue fluorescence.

As illustrated in Fig. 4, approximately absorption peak at 277 nm seen in the spectrum NS-CQDs aqueous solution referred to π-π* electronic transitions originating from sp2 hybridized carbon atom (C₌C) [41]. In addition, the excitation and emission peaks of the NS-CQDs aqueous solution were examined at different excitation wavelengths range of 300–400 nm. As excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 400 nm, the emission spectra of NS-CQDs have demonstrated a red-shift, and also when NS-CQDs excited at 350 nm, maximum emission peak was centered at 423 nm. As it is seen in the Fig. 4, the excitation wavelength significantly affects the fluorescence behavior of Ns-CQDs. This is due to the presence of possible various functional groups in NS-CQDs induced luminescent properties as well as size effect and surface defects [42–44].

The optical properties of the synthesized pure TNRs and NS-CQDs/TNRs composites have been operated by Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-Vis DRS) to verify the red shift phenomenon of samples.

The amounts of
E
g
of pristine TNRs and NS-CQDs/TNRs composites were calculated from Tauc Plot equation where
E
g
can be identified using the following equation [23];
(3)
α h ν
= A ( h ν −
E
g
)
in which α is the optical absorption coefficient, һ (6.626 ×10−34 J. Hz−1) is the Planck constant, v is the radiation frequency of the incident photon, A is the absorbance, and Eg is the semiconductor bandgap energies. In the graph drawn (αhν)2 versus hν, band gap of the material is designated when curve was extrapolated to the X-axis. According to the data presented in Fig. 5, band gap energy of naked TNRs nanoparticles was determined 3.22 eV, which is pair off with the result explained in the literature [45]. Nonetheless, the dispersion of NS-CQDs nanoparticles into the TNRs matrix has dropped off band gap energy of TNRs from 3.22 to 2.84 eV. This drop (0.38 eV) has paved the way for red shift, which may be due to C-O-Ti bonds between NS-CQDs and TNRs. As the band gap of NS-CQDs/TNRs photocomposites are reduced, power of absorption in the visible light of samples are improved, further harvesting efficiency of sunlight and causing to enhanced photocatalytic activity [46,47].

To determine the phase idendification of NS-CQDs particles and NS-CQDs/TNRs composites, we performed XRD characterization illustrated in Fig. 6. Diffraction peak at 22.31° depicted the (002) lattice planes of hexagonal graphitic carbon [48]. The main diffraction peaks located at 2θ= 25.33°, 37.95°, 48.14°, 53.89°, 54.78°, 62.76°, 68.97°, 70.22°, and 75.17° of prepared NS-CQDs/TNRs were assigned to the crystal planes of (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (215), respectively [49,50]. It also important to note that these characteristic reflection peaks are congruent with pure anatase-type of TiO2 crystal as previously reported research [33]. It was observed that XRD analysis data of pure TNRs showed similar to that of modified TNRs and did not change the intensity of peaks, which could be assumed that enough C element was not decorated with the crystal lattice of TNTs due to their dosage and crystallinity of CQDs in the fabricated nanocomposite [48,51].

The mean crystal diameter of NS-CQDs/TNRs composites were computed using typical Debye Scherer’s equation based on the position of the (101) diffraction plane [48]. The crystallite size obtained using the Scherer formula was found to be 13.67 nm, which indicated that crystallite size of NS-CQDs/TNRs hybrid composite is much smaller than that of the crystallite size of TNRs nanoparticles (24.91 nm, reported by [33].

FTIR analysis was applied to examine the molecular composition and the surface functionalities of the NS-CQDs and NS-CQDs/TNRs, as demonstrated in Fig. 7(a, b). In the FT-IR spectrum of the NS-CQDs, the broad absorption band at 3323 cm−1 attributes to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H functional groups of phenolic and amine groups [52]. Two peaks located at 2972 and 2883 cm−1 indicate to the of CH3 and CH2 groups’ stretching vibrations, resp. The peak observed at 1659 cm−1 assigned that NS-CQDs nanoparticles involve CC functional groups [53]. The stretching vibration of C-N and C-O bonds are evident from the bands at 1379 and 1048 cm−1, and also the band at 1048 cm−1 can be corresponded to C-O stretching vibrations [54,55]. The C-H stretching frequencies for NS-CQDs was observed at 1089 cm−1 [52].

As a result, these data has revealed that NS-CQDs were terrifically carbonized, and thus contain specific functional groups such as amine and hydroxyl, causing the water soluble NS-CQDs. As for NS-CQDs/TNRs, a broad characteristic peak at 3348–3236 cm−1 is ascribed to O-H stretching vibration of adsorbed water, and also slight peak at 1607 cm−1 is associated to bending of O-H groups [56]. When NS-CQDs introduced into the TNRs matrix, symmetrical stretching vibration bands of C-O-C (epoxy) bonds was observed at 1383 cm−1 [48]. Moreover, as added volume of NS-CQDs is increased, the O-H bending vibration peak are more intense. The wide absorption peak at 928 cm−1 corresponds to Ti-O stretching vibrations [57]. In accordance with the obtained FTIR results, the presence of NS-CQDs linked TNRs composites has been affirmed by increasing of band intensity and observing new absorption peaks. In this regard, TNRs might be attached with the carbon element of NS-CQDs by creating covalently bonded Ti-O-C, another thought is that oxygen in NS-CQDs is most likely linked to position incidental to oxygen in TNRs structure.

Fig. 8(a, b) shows a HRTEM image of the NS-CQDs and NS-CQDs/TNRs composites. The HRTEM image of the NS-CQDs consists of are well-dispersed without aggregation in the shape of a round sphere and demonstrates narrow size distribution in the range of 1.2–2.6 nm. The average diameter for NS-CQDs was determined to be ∼ 1.95 ± 0.97 nm for over 35 nanoparticles. The histogram in Fig. 8(a) reveals that as-prepared NS-CQDs have approximately lattice fringe of 0.318 nm, which correspond to typical (002) interlayer spacing of graphitic sp2 structure [58,59]. As illustrated in Fig. 8(b), the particles size of NS-CQDs/TNRs are mainly distributed between 4 and 12 nm and the average diameter is ∼ 8.7 ± 0.22 nm. The estimated interlayer distance was found to be 0.314 nm, which is consistent with the (110) crystal face of TiO2 [60]. It can be seen clearly that NS-CQDs/TNTs possesses a rod-like structure and displays an agglomeration.

The chemical structure and element states of the NS-CQDs nanoparticles and dot modified TNRs nanocomposites were further analyzed by XPS spectra, and as could be seen from the XPS full-scan spectra ( Fig. 9), the NS-CQDs all contained carbon (C1s, 286.0 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 400.0 eV), oxygen (O1s, 531.0 eV), sodium (Na1s 1071.0 eV), silisium (Si2p 104 eV), and potassium (K2s 377.0 eV). It has been observed that the atomic percentage of 58.15, 2.02, 20.59, 4.03, 5.76 and 6.48 % for C, N, O, Na, Si and K respectively.

The high resolution C1s spectrum of NS-CQDs (Fig. 9b) can be fitted by four peaks observed at 282.7, 286.5, 290.5 and 297.5 eV, characterized as sp2 C (C–C/CC) in graphite, the sp3 (C (C–O, or C–N), oxidized carbon (CO), and π-π* bonds [61]. XPS spectrum of N1s of NS-CQDs in Fig. 9(c) could be deconvoluted into only one peak at 397.4 eV, corresponding to N-(CO)- [62]. Fig. 9(d) exhibits the O1s XPS spectrum of NS-CQDs with three binding energy peaks located at 529.5, 532.4, and 536.2 eV, which were identified as Ti-O, C-OH/OC-O, O-CO, respectively [63,64]. Considering that data belonging to CO, C-O and C-N in FTIR spectrum, it can be inferred that FTIR results well consistent with the XPS data.

The survey spectra of NS-CQDs/TNRs has exhibited three distinct characteristic peaks of C, O, and Ti, which have binding energies 286.0, 529.0, and 459.0 eV shown in Fig. 10 (a), respectively. The relative percentage of the elements C, O, and Ti were 17.04 %, 55.42 %, and 19.14 %, respectively. When C1s was deconvoluted (Fig. 10 b), it was observed that three kinds of carbon involving groups was revealed at 282.9, 286.1, and 292.6 eV, representing C-C, C-O-C, and saturated π-π* bonds, resp. [65]. For O1s (Fig. 10 c), the peak divides into two peaks, i.e. 529.7 eV and 531.7 eV, which could be corresponded to Ti-O and CO in chemical state, marking the formation of Ti-O- C bonds between NS-CQDs and TNRs [66]. As depicted in the Ti2p spectra (Fig. 10 d), the peaks located at 458.9 eV and 464.9 eV belonged to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, separately [18]. The spin energy separation was 6.0 eV, implying the dominant Ti4+ cation in the form of TiO2. It is very important to mention that the shift of 6.0 eV is caused to change chemical environment of Ti in the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic removal of MB, Cr (VI) and inactivation of e-Coli and A. niger
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic properties of the prepared NS-CQDs/TNRs photocomposites under visible light (UV-A, 365 nm) were evaluated MB, Cr (VI), E. coli and A. niger was chosen as target contaminants in the photoremoval and photo-inactivation experiments. Prior to photoremoval and inactivation experiments, dark adsorption were carried out for 30 min to discount the influence of catalysts adsorption of pollutants in the photocatalysis process. Because analytical data was shared in the previous study [33], the photocatalytic effect of pure TNRs on MB and Cr (VI) has not been reinvestigated. ıt was benefitted from all NS-CQDs/TNRs-X nanomaterials. However, it would rather NS-CQDs(20)/TNRs composite, which is the corresponding product presents the best photocatalytic removal of MB and Cr (VI). Moreover, this composite was evaluated for photo-inactivation and all characterization steps. As can be seen in Fig. 11, photo-induced MB degradation efficiency is up to 94.3 % in 150 min with NS-CQDs/TNRs as the photocatalyst compared to that of adsorption value (36.9 %). Whereas, 60.3 % of MB was degraded in the presence of TNRs and only 32.9 % of MB was occurred by way of adsorption (M. Koç Keşir, 2024). The degradation efficiency of all composites observed the order as NS-CQDs(20)/TNRs (94.3 %) > NS-CQDs(5)/TNRs (89.5 %) > NS-CQDs(25)/TNRs (88.7 %) > NS-CQDs(15)/TNRs (83.6 %) > NS-CQDs(10)/TNRs (83.4 %) > NS-CQDs(1)/TNRs (80.1 %).

Among these composites, NS-CQDs(20)/TNRs have exhibited best photocatalytic performance, which may be inferred that proper volume of NS-CQDs embedded on TNRs fosters remarkably the efficiency of MB photocatalytic degradation. It is worth emphasizing that the band gap of NS-CQDs/TNRs (2.84 eV) was much power than that of TNRs (3.22 eV) calculated in UV-DRS (Fig. 5), implying that absorption band of NS-CQDs/TNRs is shifted to red region in UV–vis spectrum and could affect the optical property of TNRs, significantly developing the absorption of visible light that of TNRs [66]. Furthermore, the outstanding photocatalytic performance of NS-CQDs(20)/TNRs based on collaboration between CQDs and TNRs could be due to the improved light-harvesting capacity, as validated in the photoluminescence and UV–vis DRS analysis presented Figs. 4–5 [20].

Photocatalytic potential of the synthesized NS-CQDS/TNRs was investigated in the reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) besides photodegradation of MB. The pH of the 10 ppm Cr (VI) solution was adjusted to approximately 3.0 by using HCl and NaOH solutions. According to the Purboix Curve of Cr (VI), Cr (VI) in an aqueous solution can be found in different species including
H
2
Cr
O
4
(aq.) and
HCr
O
4
−
below pH 6.5, and
Cr
O
4
2 −
above pH 6.5 [61]. Value of pH is important because it causes both the isoelectric point of the photocatalyst and the Cr(VI) species in aquatic environment. Surface of NS-CQDs/TNRs is positively charged at pH:3 lower than
pH
pzc
(the point of zero charge, 7.18) and mostly
HCr
O
4
−
ions are situated at this pH, increasing the photocatalytic reduction is due to coulomb attraction force between positively charged composite and negatively charged Cr (VI) species. Fig. 12 was illustrated that photoreduction efficiency of NS-CQDs(25)/TNRs, NS-CQDs(20)/TNRs, NS-CQDs(15)/TNRs, NS-CQDs(10)/TNRs, NS-CQDs(1)/TNRs, and NS-CQDs(5)/TNRs for Cr (VI) after 150 min of photocatalytic reaction were 85.5 %, 85.1 %, 83.9 %, 74.5 %, 72.8 % and 72.5 %, respectively. The photo reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) increased from 72.8 % to 85.1 % when volume of NS-CQDs increases from 1 to 20 mL. Only pristine TNRs without NS-CQDs has reduced about 51.5 % of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) after 150 min irradiation. But, the higher volume of NS-CQDs penetrated into the TNRs could decline the generation of
e
−
/
h
+
pairs due to inhibition of radiation coming to the catalyst [57]. It was seen that upon rising up to 25 mL of NS-CQDs, percentage reduction of Cr (VI) (85.5 %) is not considerable changed, so it was decide to utilize the photo-inactivation, cytotoxicity assay and characterizations. As depicted Figs. 11–12, As soon as the TNRs catalysts equipped with NS-CQDs and the MB/Cr (VI) solution comes together, it has observed that removal rate was abruptly drop-off at 15 min. under dark condition. This abnormal behavior may be because of fast adsorption of Cr (VI) ions and MB molecules [20].

Fig. 13 depicted the reusability of the NS-CQDS/TNRs photocomposites both MB and Cr (VI). It was operated four consecutive runs for MB, and results illustrated that composites are partly deactivated. Fig. 13 (right) exhibits that about 5.5 % drop in photodegradation of MB was come from cycles 1 to cycle 4, which might be due to loss of the composites originated from regeneration experiments. But still, fabricated NS-CQDs/TNRs composites have good recyclability and stability during photocatalytic reactions. However, in the reusability experiments for Cr (VI), it was seen dramatically dipping in reduction up to cycles 4 in Fig. 13 (left). It is observed that the photocatalytic activity of nanocomposite decreased dramatically by 12.5 % at four cycles. The reason could be explained that Cr (III) occurred with reduction of Cr (VI) accumulates on the surface of the NS-CQDs/TNRs composites because the regenerating step, especially HNO3 dipping, was not sufficient.

The antifungal and antibacterial properties of NS-CQDs/TNRs against the E. coli and A. niger has been displayed in Fig. 14. Photo-inactivation behavior of TNRs has been reported in previous research [33]. E. coli are almost inactivated in the NS-CQDs/TNRs in 30 min of visible light irradiation, whereas some colonies of E. coli are still retained in the presence of TNRs. The over-apparent rise in inactivation may be that existence of NS-CQDs extends the light absorption to the visible light region and thus the formation of reactive oxygen species (such as
H
O ′
,
H
2
O
2
,
O
2
⋅ −
), which attacks the cell wall of E. coli and oxidizes lipopolysaccharide bilayer, phospholipid layer, and peptidoglycan in bacteria cells is intensified [67]. According to the antifungal results, it was not observed decrease in the number of fungal spores. The charge interaction between NS-CQDs/TNRs and A. niger depends on critically the structure of the catalyst and the chemistry of the A. niger surface [30]. Another plausible reason for inactivation A. niger could be that A. niger is directly dependent on the UVA light intensity. C. Pokhum vd.,2017 reported that intracellular organelles of A. niger spore were not affected by nanomaterials and UVA radiation, even though the outermost melanin-like layer and cell wall were damaged [68].

The toxic properties of NS-CQDs and hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs composite against MCF-7 cancer cells and ARPE-19 cells were evaluated by MTT method. It was observed that the NS-CQDs and hybrid NS-CQDs/TNRs composite at concentrations of 0–500 μg/mL did not have a toxic effect on MCF-7 cancer and ARPE-19 cells ( Fig. 15).

In summary, green synthesized CQDs from nutshell as a carbon source were used in functionalized of TNRs surface so as to prepare a new composite NS-CQDs/TNRs. Fix-bed pyrolysis and wet impregnation method were employed to synthesize NS-CQDs and NS-CQDs modified TNRs. Subsequently it was verified their structure, morphology, and optical properties using further spectroscopic techniques. Compared to pristine TNRs, it has been observed that the NS-CQDs/TNRs composite has higher photocatalytic removal of MB, Cr (VI), and E. coli save for A. niger. The NS-CQDs(20)/TNRs composite showed more excellent photocatalytic performance than that of other TNRs composites including different volume of CQDs. Moreover, the results revealed that NS-CQDs(20)/TNRs composite showed the best inactivation performance for E. coli removal whereas it exhibited negative effect in visible light-driven fungal inactivation of A. niger. Taken into account for cytotoxicity data, nanocomposite and NS-CQDs molecules were non-toxic with respect to environmental and biological safety. This work will induce to great potential for utilization of bio-waste in the synthesis new class of materials towards photocatalytic applications.
